cards.back: Yes. Constexpr functions are implicitly inline, which means the compiler can see the full function definition and potentially inline the function call.

cards.text: For a constexpr function to be evaluated at compile-time, the compiler must be able to see the {{c1::full definition}} of the function.

cards.front: Can you call a constexpr function at compile-time using only its forward declaration?
cards.back: No. The compiler needs to see the full definition of a constexpr function to evaluate it at compile-time. A forward declaration alone is not sufficient.

cards.text: Constexpr function definitions are typically placed in {{c1::header files}} so they can be {{c2::included wherever they are needed}}.

cards.front: Can function parameters in a constexpr function be constexpr?
cards.back: No. Function parameters cannot be constexpr, even in a constexpr function. They can be const, but not constexpr.

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Function parameters cannot be constexpr. Remove the constexpr keyword from the parameters.
cards.explanation: Even in a constexpr function, parameters can only be const, not constexpr. The correct declaration is: constexpr int add(int x, int y)

cards.text: Local variables in a constexpr function can be {{c1::const}} but not {{c2::constexpr}} (unless their initializers are constant expressions).

cards.front: What operations are NOT allowed in constexpr functions?
cards.back:
• Non-constexpr function calls (when evaluating at compile-time)
  • I/O operations (std::cout, std::cin)
  • Accessing non-const global/static variables
  • Operations with undefined behavior

cards.concept: When to make a function constexpr
cards.explanation:
Make a function constexpr if:
  • It performs calculations that could be done at compile-time
  • It doesn't perform I/O operations
  • It doesn't rely on runtime-only features
  • You want to use it in constant expressions
Best practice: If a function CAN be constexpr, it SHOULD be constexpr.
examples: constexpr int square(int x) { return x * x; }  // Good candidate
void print(int x) { std::cout << x; }  // Cannot be constexpr (I/O)

cards.back: Yes. Constexpr functions can call themselves recursively, which is useful for algorithms like factorial calculation.

cards.question: Will this evaluate at compile-time or runtime?
cards.explanation: The function is constexpr, the argument (5) is a constant expression, and result is constexpr, requiring compile-time evaluation. The compiler will calculate 120 at compile-time.
